content: Telemedicine encompasses remote healthcare delivery via technology: (1) Synchronous video visits - real-time provider-patient interaction; (2) Asynchronous (store-and-forward) - transmitting images/data for later review (dermatology, radiology); (3) Remote patient monitoring - transmitting vital signs, glucose, weight from home; (4) Mobile health (mHealth) - smartphone apps for symptom tracking, medication reminders; (5) E-consults - specialist consultation without patient visit. Telehealth utilization surged from <1% pre-pandemic to 30-40% of visits during COVID-19 peaks, stabilizing at 10-15% post-pandemic. Evidence shows telemedicine effective for chronic disease management, mental health, dermatology, and follow-up visits, with high patient satisfaction (75-85%).

Effective telemedicine implementation requires: (1) Technology infrastructure - broadband access, user-friendly platforms, EHR integration; (2) Workflow redesign - pre-visit tech checks, virtual waiting rooms, appropriate triage (which conditions suitable for telehealth); (3) Reimbursement parity - CMS and private payers now covering telehealth at similar rates to in-person (temporary COVID waivers made permanent for many services); (4) Hybrid care models - combining in-person and virtual visits based on clinical need; (5) Addressing digital divide - providing devices, data plans, tech support for underserved populations. Studies show telehealth reduces no-show rates (from 15-20% to 5-10%), improves chronic disease control (HbA1c reduction 0.5-1.0%), and increases access for rural/mobility-limited patients.

Telemedicine appropriateness criteria: Suitable for - medication management, mental health therapy, chronic disease follow-up, minor acute conditions (UTI, conjunctivitis), dermatology (with good images), post-operative checks. Not suitable for - chest pain, severe abdominal pain, trauma, conditions requiring physical exam (joint effusion, heart murmurs), procedures. Limitations include: inability to perform hands-on examination, technology barriers for elderly/low-literacy patients, licensing restrictions across state/country borders, variable audio/video quality affecting diagnostic accuracy. Studies show diagnostic concordance 80-90% with in-person for appropriate conditions, but higher rates of antibiotic prescribing for respiratory infections (potential overprescribing). Equity concerns: 20-30% lower telehealth use among elderly, limited English proficiency, and lowest income quintile.
